front: Event: founded by the semi-mythical "Great Peacemaker" (Deganawidah), assisted by Hiawatha, who together instituted the Great Law of Peace
work: Founding: the Great Peacemaker, Hiawatha, and Jigonsaseh

front: Event: co-founded by Jigonsaseh, revered as the "Mother of Nations"
work: Founding: the Great Peacemaker, Hiawatha, and Jigonsaseh

front: Event: founding legend holds that its leaders uprooted a pine tree and cast all their weapons into the hole
work: Founding: the Great Peacemaker, Hiawatha, and Jigonsaseh

front: Event: founding was driven partly by fear of the neighboring Algonquians
work: Founding: the Great Peacemaker, Hiawatha, and Jigonsaseh

front: Figure: its legendary first leader, the Great Peacemaker, reportedly suffered from a severe speech impediment
work: Founding: the Great Peacemaker, Hiawatha, and Jigonsaseh

front: Document: an oral constitution codified by Dekanawidah and Hiawatha, recorded on wampum belts
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Document: amending it was called "adding to the rafters"
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Document: gives women the right to confirm or deny inheritance of political offices, symbolized by their control over strings of shells
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Document: permits granting an advisory office to worthy non-hereditary leaders, who are likened to newly-sprung pine trees
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Document: symbolized by four joined white squares with a white pine in the middle, originally depicted on a wampum belt
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Institution: the centrally located Onondaga were granted the title "Keepers of the Fire," hosting the Grand Council's eternal fire
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Institution: subgroups were designated "Keepers of the Western Door," "Keepers of the Eastern Door," and "Keepers of the Central Fire"
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Institution: representatives had to publicly debate proposals to unanimity within their "moiety" before other constituencies could vote
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Institution: chiefs (sachems) could be demoted by clan mothers in a process called "knocking off the horns"
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: Institution: "Condolence Canes" were used to record succession among its rulers
work: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure

front: War: fought France and its Huron allies for control of the fur trade, using guns sold by the Dutch
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: War: killed Jesuit missionaries including Isaac Jogues, John de Brebeuf, Charles Garnier, and Gabriel Lallemant, some later canonized as North American Martyrs
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: War: fifty captured members were sent to Marseilles as galley slaves after being taken at Fort Frontenac
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: War: was defeated by the French at the Battle of Sorel, after which Louis-Hector de Calliere negotiated peace
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: Event: early on, displaced the Quapaw, Mosopelea, and Tutelo peoples and defeated the Neutral Nation
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: Event: expanded into the Ohio Country from its upstate New York base after trading furs to Europeans for firearms
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: Practice: replaced fallen family members by adopting captives taken in raids called "Mourning Wars"
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: Event: the Susquehannock, though speakers of an Iroquoian language, never joined the confederacy and instead fought it during the Beaver Wars
work: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion

front: Event: signed a series of agreements with British colonists called the Covenant Chain
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Concept: "kaswentha" refers to the relationship negotiated to coexist with foreign powers
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Event: a treaty pledging peace "as long as the sun shines upon the earth" and "the waters flow" was metaphorically named for a link to a sailing ship
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Event: four "kings," including Hendrick Peters (erroneously called "Emperor"), went on a diplomatic mission to Great Britain, later painted as three of the "Four Kings of the New World"
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Event: ceded parts of its western territory in the Nanfan Treaty
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Figure: a German-born diplomat (Conrad Weiser) earned the title "Holder of the Heavens" for negotiating peace treaties alongside James Logan
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Figure: the "half-king" Tanacharison conducted trade and diplomacy at Logstown before his arrest by Governor Duquesne
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Event: ceded claims to land in Virginia and Maryland at the 1744 Treaty of Lancaster, aided by British negotiator Thomas Bladen
work: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties

front: Concept: a 1988 U.S. Senate resolution recognized the Great Law of Peace for its influence on the ideals of the U.S. Constitution
work: The Iroquois Influence Thesis

front: Concept: Cadwallader Colden's earliest academic study of the confederacy noted its "great men" were poorer than its "common people"
work: The Iroquois Influence Thesis

front: Work: Donald Grinde and Bruce Johansen's Exemplar of Liberty defended the controversial "influence thesis" about the confederacy's impact on American government
work: The Iroquois Influence Thesis

front: Event: a leader's speech at the Treaty of Lancaster apocryphally inspired the plan to unify the colonies at the Albany Congress
work: The Iroquois Influence Thesis

front: Practice: the prophet Handsome Lake spread a Quaker-influenced revitalization movement called Gai'wiio, or the Longhouse Religion, still practiced by the Seneca
work: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake

front: Practice: the Longhouse Religion encoded its teachings in a wampum language legendarily invented by Hiawatha
work: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake

front: Concept: J.N.B. Hewitt described belief in "otgun," a nefarious counterpart to the mana-like concept of "orenda"
work: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake

front: Work: Arthur C. Parker translated the "Good Message," describing an "evil one" who lived in a gold castle and sent abortion and witchcraft
work: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake

front: Practice: after deaths, held a condolence ceremony; healers may belong to the mask-wearing False Face Society
work: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake

front: Figure: Catherine Tekakwitha, associated with the mission at Kahnawake, became the first canonized American Indian saint
work: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake

front: Figure: mostly allied with the British during the American Revolution under Mohawk leader Joseph Brant
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: Event: divided loyalties among member nations during the Revolution produced the Battle of Oriskany
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: Event: the Cherry Valley Massacre, carried out by confederacy warriors and Loyalists, prompted the Continental Congress to order an attack culminating in the Battle of Newtown
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: Event: confederacy warriors allied with Loyalist forces in the Wyoming Massacre
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: War: in response, George Washington (called the "Town Destroyer") ordered the 1779 Sullivan-Clinton Expedition, a scorched-earth campaign that broke confederacy power in western New York
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: Event: after the Revolution, pro-British members relocated to the Haldimand Grant in Ontario under the 1784 Treaty of Fort Stanwix
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: Work: the rivalry between missionary Samuel Kirkland and a confederacy leader is documented in Alan Taylor's book The Divided Ground
work: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition

front: Figure: Ely Parker, a member of the confederacy, wrote the instrument of Confederate surrender at Appomattox Court House
work: Aftermath: Ely Parker and 19th-Century Legal Cases

front: Case: Ely Parker prevailed against the Ogden Land Company in the 1857 case Fellows v. Blacksmith over the Treaty of Buffalo Creek
work: Aftermath: Ely Parker and 19th-Century Legal Cases

front: Figure: Lewis Henry Morgan performed extensive fieldwork on the confederacy, naming his "bifurcate merging" kinship-system typology after them
work: Anthropological Study and Literary Depictions

front: Work: Anthony Wallace wrote the paper "Revitalization Movements" after fieldwork studying the confederacy and its religion
work: Anthropological Study and Literary Depictions

front: Work: in James Fenimore Cooper's Leatherstocking Tales, they are depicted as antagonists led by the character Magua in The Last of the Mohicans, and capture Hurry Harry and Thomas Hutter in The Deerslayer
work: Anthropological Study and Literary Depictions
